data.title: Celsius Confident in 2025 Outlook, Should Benefit from Alani Nu Acquisition — Market Talk
data.description: Celsius' $1.8 billion buy of Alani Nu should complement the company, William Blair analysts say in a research note, citing the rapidly growing energy drink brand's appeal to female consumers, as well as its relevance with Gen Z and Millennial consumers. The deal, which is expected to be accretive to per-share earnings within a year, will also alleviate competition risks, they add. The acquisition comes as Celsius says it is confident in its prospects for 2025, despite not providing earnings guidance. The company is launching new flavors, expanding into new categories and seeking to increase its distribution, which the analysts say should add to its already attractive position. Celsius surges 26% following a 4Q earnings beat. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Bank of Canada CPI-Targeting Review Won't Touch 2% Level, Macklem Says — Update
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--The Bank of Canada has begun work on next year's renewal of its inflation-targeting framework, although Gov. Tiff Macklem on Friday rejected any talk of changing the target level from 2%.

Officials are focusing instead on whether policymakers have the right gauges of core inflation in place, and a closer look at whether shelter costs--fueled by a housing shortage in Canada--are distorting certain inflation readings, said Macklem, according to prepared remarks set for delivery in Mississauga, Ontario.

Since 1995, the Bank of Canada and Department of Finance have agreed on a mandate that compels central-bank policymakers to set interest rates to achieve and maintain 2% inflation, and that framework is reviewed and renewed every five years. The last renewal was in late 2021.

In each of the five reviews since the mid-1990s, central bank and government officials agreed that 2% inflation was the appropriate target, even after considering alternatives, Macklem said.

"Canadians have told us they don't want higher inflation," Macklem said. "They have also told us that the 2% target is well known and well understood."

"With trade conflict on our doorstep, we need to focus our resources on the most pressing and important issues for our framework review," he said. "In my view, now is not the time to question the anchor that has proven so effective in achieving price stability," he added.

Macklem laid out the expected priorities in the pending inflation-targeting review. Chief among them is to examine the three measures of core inflation presently preferred by the central bank. He said researchers will determine whether the current approach is satisfactory, or whether a "broader approach" is better.

He said the review would consider the interaction between rate policy and housing. Some economists argued that the Bank of Canada kept interest rates elevated for too long a period, because shelter costs skewed the data. When housing was excluded, those economists said, inflation was sharply lower and should have likely triggered earlier rate cuts from the Bank of Canada.

Macklem said housing affordability is a major headwind in the Canadian economy, fueled by a shortage of residential units across the country that runs into the millions.

"Monetary policy cannot directly increase housing supply," Macklem said. "We must consider how monetary policy affects housing demand and supply and how the imbalance between them feeds into inflation in shelter prices."

Macklem also raised the possibility that housing-market imbalances might be distorting the central bank's preferred core-inflation readings.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: US Stocks Slip on Weak Data and Inflation Worries
data.description:
Stocks in the US fell sharply on Friday as weak economic data and concerns over inflation weighed on investor sentiment.

The S&P 500 lost nearly 1%, while the Dow Jones dropped over 500 points, and the Nasdaq 100 slipped 1.1%.

UnitedHealth shares plunged more than 8% following reports that the Department of Justice is investigating its Medicare billing practices, making it the worst-performing Dow component.

Consumer sentiment also took a hit, with the University of Michigan’s index falling to 64.7, reflecting growing concerns over inflation, which consumers now expect to rise to 4.3% in the coming year.

Walmart’s (-3%) warning about potential price hikes due to tariff uncertainty further dampened market confidence, contributing to Thursday’s broader market downturn.

While the S&P 500 and Nasdaq are still on track for weekly gains, the Dow is facing a loss, signaling increased volatility amid economic uncertainty.

data.title: Apple Pulls Advanced Data Protection Service in U.K.
data.description:
By Edith Hancock

Apple removed its Advanced Data Protection feature in the U.K. after the government reportedly requested access to user data stored on its iCloud service.

The company said on Friday that the service--which ensures only users can access files such as photos and documents that are encrypted on Apple's iCloud storage--will no longer be available to new users and current U.K. users will need to eventually disable the security feature.

"We are gravely disappointed that the protections provided by ADP will not be available to our customers in the U.K. given the continuing rise of data breaches and other threats to customer privacy," a spokesperson for the company said, saying that securing personal data access in the cloud with end-to-end encryption is "more urgent than ever."

The move comes after The Washington Post and BBC reported earlier this month that U.K. government officials asked Apple to create a so-called back door that gives them access to content any Apple user uploads to the cloud.

"Apple remains committed to offering our users the highest level of security for their personal data and are hopeful that we will be able to do so in the future in the United Kingdom," they said. "As we have said many times before, we have never built a backdoor or master key to any of our products or services and we never will."

Write to Edith Hancock at edith.hancock@wsj.com

data.title: SouthState Corp SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
SouthState Corporation, a financial holding company headquartered in Winter Haven, Florida, has released its annual 10-K report, detailing its financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's activities and future outlook, reflecting its commitment to growth and market expansion.

Financial Highlights

SouthState Corporation reported the following key financial metrics for the year:

  • Total Revenue: $1,717.7 million, reflecting a slight decrease from the previous year due to changes in interest income and noninterest income.
  • Net Income: $534.8 million, an increase of 8.2% compared to the previous year, driven by higher interest income and improved economic forecasts.
  • Diluted EPS: $6.97, up 7.9% from the previous year, indicating improved profitability per share.
  • Net Interest Income: $1,415.5 million, a decrease of 2.6% from the previous year, primarily due to increased interest expenses outpacing interest income growth.
  • Noninterest Income: $302.3 million, an increase of 5.4% from the previous year, driven by higher mortgage banking income and trust and investment services income.

Business Highlights

SouthState Corporation's business operations for the year included the following highlights:

  • Company Overview: SouthState Corporation operates through its subsidiary, SouthState Bank, with a 251-branch network across six states, providing a wide range of banking services.
  • Correspondent Banking and Capital Markets: The Bank serves over 1,200 community financial institutions, generating revenue from fixed income security sales, hedging services, and loan brokerage fees.
  • Broker Dealer Operations: SouthState|DuncanWilliams Securities Corp. complements the Bank’s capital markets business, offering additional opportunities to the Bank’s client base.
  • Investment Advisory Services: SouthState Advisory, Inc. supports the Bank’s Wealth line of business, offering financial planning and investment management services.
  • Corporate Billing Division: The Bank’s transaction-based finance division provides factoring, invoicing, and accounts receivable management services nationwide, focusing on transportation and automotive sectors.
  • New Investment Subsidiary: In late 2023, the Bank formed SSB First Street Corporation to hold tax-exempt municipal investment securities, enhancing its investment portfolio.
  • Loan Portfolio Composition: As of December 31, 2024, the loan portfolio includes 53% commercial real estate loans, 26% residential real estate loans, 18% commercial and industrial loans, and 3% consumer loans.
  • Deposit Products and Services: The Bank offers a variety of deposit products, including checking, savings, and money market accounts, with a focus on capturing additional retail and commercial deposits.
  • Wealth Management Services: The Bank provides wealth management services targeting affluent clients, offering financial planning, retirement services, and a range of investment alternatives.
  • Mortgage Banking: The Bank’s mortgage line originates single-family home loans, selling them into the secondary market or holding them in its portfolio, retaining servicing rights.
  • Acquisition Strategy: The Company focuses on strategic mergers and acquisitions that align with its principles of soundness, profitability, and growth, enhancing its footprint and market diversification.
  • Human Capital Management: The Company emphasizes a strong relationship with employees, offering competitive compensation, benefits, and a team-oriented environment, with a focus on diversity and inclusion.
  • Core Values and Culture: The Company’s core values include local market leadership, long-term horizon, remarkable experiences, meaningful relationships, and a greater purpose, guiding employee interactions and customer service.
  • Corporate Stewardship: The Company is committed to equality, inclusion, and workplace diversity, with initiatives to promote a diverse and inclusive workplace and support community development.
  • Regulatory Environment: The Company is subject to extensive federal and state regulation, with a focus on maintaining compliance with capital requirements, consumer protection laws, and anti-money laundering rules.
  • Future Outlook: The Company expects to continue assessing acquisition opportunities and adapting to regulatory changes, with a focus on strategic growth and maintaining a strong capital position.

Strategic Initiatives

SouthState Corporation has undertaken several strategic initiatives to enhance its market position and financial strength:

  • Acquisition of Independent Bank Group, Inc.: The company completed the acquisition of Independent Bank Group, Inc., a Texas-based corporation, in a stock transaction valued at $2.5 billion, aimed at expanding its footprint and market presence.
  • Sale-Leaseback Transaction: The company entered into a sale-leaseback transaction with Blue Owl Real Estate Capital LLC, involving certain bank branch properties, to optimize its real estate portfolio and improve liquidity. The transaction is expected to close in the first quarter of 2025, subject to satisfactory due diligence by Blue Owl.
  • Stock Repurchase Program: The company received supervisory nonobjection from the Federal Reserve Board for its 2025 stock repurchase program, authorizing the repurchase of up to 3,000,000 shares, approximately 3% of its outstanding shares.
  • Capital Position: SouthState Corporation maintained a strong capital position with a total risk-based capital ratio of 15.0% and a Tier 1 leverage ratio of 10.0% as of December 31, 2024. The company paid dividends totaling $161.6 million during the year, reflecting a dividend payout ratio of 30.22%.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue its strategic growth through both organic means and potential future acquisitions, leveraging its strong capital position to explore strategic opportunities that align with its long-term growth objectives.

Challenges and Risks

SouthState Corporation faces several challenges and risks, including:

  • Merger Integration Risks: The company faces significant risks related to its merger with IBTX, including integration challenges, potential loss of key employees, and increased compliance and legal risks.
  • Economic and Regulatory Risks: The company’s growth strategy is subject to risks from economic conditions, regulatory changes, and competitive pressures. Failure to manage growth effectively could adversely affect financial results.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to interest rate fluctuations, inflationary pressures, and increased competition for deposits, which could impact net interest income, profitability, and liquidity.
  • Operational Risks: Technological changes, including the adoption of artificial intelligence, require significant investments to remain competitive. The company’s reliance on real estate-secured loans exposes it to risks from potential declines in real estate markets.
  • Regulatory Risks: The company faces heightened expectations from regulatory agencies, compliance with evolving laws, and potential increases in FDIC insurance premiums. The ability to raise additional capital may be constrained by market conditions and regulatory requirements.
  • Emerging Risks: The adoption and use of AI tools introduce concerns around safety, fairness, and compliance with laws. The company’s AI governance framework aims to manage these risks, but rapid technological advancements and regulatory changes pose ongoing challenges.
  • Management Strategies: Management acknowledges the challenges of integrating IBTX and achieving expected synergies. The company is focused on strategic initiatives to enhance digital banking capabilities and improve risk management frameworks.

SEC Filing: SouthState Corp [ SSB ] - 10-K - Feb. 21, 2025

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Friday at 1 PM ET
data.description:
Home Sales Fell 4.9% in January, Extending Slump in Housing Market

Home prices and mortgage rates, which were a drag on home sales for most of 2024, stayed elevated and put off many buyers.

U.S. Consumer Confidence Falls Back on Fears of Tariff-Induced Price Increases

Inflation expectations jumped to 4.3% this month from 3.3% in January, the highest reading since 2023.

UnitedHealth Group Drop Pulls Dow Industrials Lower

The Dow fell moderately on probe of UnitedHealth by the Justice Department, while the S&P 500 and Nasdaq-100 posted smaller declines. Sales of existing homes fell 4.9%.

Europe's Economic Stagnation Persists, Latest Business Surveys Say

The eurozone economy continued to flatline in February, torn between signs of revival in Germany and sharp decline in France.

Chinese Stocks Rally on Strong Alibaba Earnings, DeepSeek Optimism

Upbeat earnings by big tech companies have added to a recent rally driven by Xi Jinping's meeting with top corporate executives and the AI hype around DeepSeek.

In the Trump Era, Flying Green Doesn't Matter Anymore

The president's endorsement has pushed up air-taxi stocks, but development of all types of zero-emission aircraft is falling by the wayside.

U.K. Retail Sales Rise for First Time in Five Months

Retailers in the U.K. reported a brighter start to the new year, with sales rising after four straight months of falls, largely from a recovery in the food segment.

French Industry Sentiment Improves Despite Tariff Threat

The mood in France's factories brightened a little this month as borrowing costs come down, and despite the threat of tariffs on exports to the U.S.

Musk Looks at Auditing the Federal Reserve

The billionaire gives remarks at the Conservative Political Action Conference.

Why Is Warren Buffett Hoarding So Much Cash?

Investors are poised to study the Berkshire Hathaway chairman's annual letter for insights about the stock market.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Friday at 1 PM ET
data.description:
DOJ Investigates Medicare Billing Practices at UnitedHealth

The Justice Department is probing diagnoses and practices that triggered extra payments to UnitedHealth's Medicare Advantage plans, people familiar with the matter say.

FDA Declares Shortage of Novo Nordisk's Wegovy, Ozempic Over

The FDA announcement confirms that the U.S. supply of both prescription-only drugs now meets or exceeds the current and projected demand in the country, the Danish pharmaceutical company said.

Rivian keeps promise on making a profit, but here's why the stock is falling

Rivian Automotive Inc. makes good on its promise to get to its first quarterly gross profit and blows past Wall Street estimates for its sales.

Coinbase Says SEC Intends to Drop Lawsuit Against Crypto Exchange

The agency had sought to oversee the company as a stock market.

Moody's Cuts Nissan Motor's Rating to Junk Status

Moody's said the rating action reflects the Japanese automaker's weak profitability driven by slowing demand for its aging model portfolio.

Trump Media Approves Fund for Mergers, Acquisitions, Partnerships

Trump Media & Technology Group looks to expand tech footprint as it branches out into finance and other industries.

Trump Is Planning to Take Control of the Postal Service, Officials Say

The president is seeking to disband the mail provider's governing board and put the USPS under the control of the Commerce Department.

Thyssenkrupp Says Potential Buyer of HKM Joint Venture Broke Off Talks

The industrial group said discussions with CE Capital Partners about buying Huettenwerke Krupp Mannesmann had collapsed.

The Toughest Job in Fashion? Design Head for a Luxury Brand.

High-end brands are churning through creative directors as they try to revive sales.

U.K. Fines Banks Over Information Sharing on Government Bonds

The competition regulator fined Citi, HSBC, Morgan Stanley and Royal Bank of Canada $132 million after a probe into the exchange of sensitive information about government bonds.

data.title: UBS Expects Strong Nvidia Earnings, Forecasts Blackwell Ramp-Up in 2025
data.description:
UBS analysts expect Nvidia (NVDA, Financials) to report strong earnings, with revenue and data center performance meeting or exceeding investor expectations.

Based on data center sector contribution of $38.5 billion to $39 billion, the company anticipates fiscal first-quarter revenue guidance to be between $42.5 billion and $43 billion.

A major modification in Nvidia's revenue recognition methodology will show income from Blackwell compute boards at shipping instead of depending only on OEM/ODM partner whole rack sales. Given computing board sales rising ahead of complete GB200 rack shipments, analysts think Nvidia is controlling a short-term supply chain imbalance.

Customers value availability over waiting for GB200 racks, so high demand is pushing a change toward Blackwell SKUs like as the B200 on the air-cooled HGX platform. With estimates of Blackwell-related income in the fourth quarter at over $9 billion, UBS projects that it might more than quadruple to over $20 billion in the first quarter, corresponding to roughly 700,000 units delivered.

Ahead of a larger GB200 rack launch, which UBS expects to spike in March 2025, Nvidia is likely to keep supplying computing boards. End hyperscale customers help to partly fund certain OEM/ODM inventory finance for Blackwell.

Forecasting $234 billion in 2025 sales and an earnings per share projection of $5.33, UBS has a Buy recommendation on Nvidia, with possible rises to $6.25 or higher in 2026. For the shares, UBS has a target price of $185.

data.title: Walmart Price Target Rises to $115, Analysts Eye Pullback Opportunity
data.description:
Feb 21 - TD Cowen raises Walmart's (WMT, Financial) stock price target to $115 as analysts see growth potential despite a sharp decline. TD Cowen shows renewed confidence in Walmart after the retailer posted Q4 earnings per share of 66 cents, narrowly beating the Street's 65?cent expectation. The share drop followed cautious guidance for the first quarter of fiscal year 2026, with projected EPS between 57 and 58 cents and a full?year forecast of $2.50 to $2.60, below anticipated figures.

Analysts see the drop in the share price as an opportunity to buy, as the company has strong fundamentals. The retailer's growth strategy is to grow its grocery businesses, value offerings, supply chain efficiency, and high margin businesses like its marketplace and ad services. Moreover, other market analysts such as RBC Capital Markets, BMO Capital Markets as well as others have mixed projections. The upgrade by TD Cowen anticipates a long-run picture as Walmart deals with near-term difficulties while profiting from its operational suitability.

data.title: Bank of Canada Gov. Macklem Warns of 'Severe' Fallout From Trade Row With U.S.
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canada's economy is on solid footing but the breakout of a protracted trade conflict with the U.S. would have "severe" consequences, Bank of Canada Gov. Tiff Macklem said Friday.

Canada won a reprieve this month on President Trump's threat to impose 25% tariffs on the country's nonenergy imports, and a 10% tariff on energy products entering the U.S. That reprieve lasts until March 4, and Trump said his decision would depend on Canada's progress in deterring fentanyl trafficking and reaching a deal on certain economic issues.

The Bank of Canada issues its next rate-policy decision on March 12. Macklem said Canada's economy "is on a better footing," according to prepared remarks set for delivery in Mississauga, Ontario, or west of Toronto. He cited a solid pickup in consumer spending, fueled by aggressive rate cuts since June of last year. Earlier Friday, Statistics Canada reported a 2.5% increase in retail sales in December, the biggest increase in about two years.

Financial markets have scaled back rate-cut expectations for Canada based on the firmer data and a view that Trump's threat of hefty tariffs on all Canadian imports won't materialize.

Macklem said the "consequences could be severe" should Trump follow through on his hefty Canadian tariffs, and the government in Ottawa retaliates as it has promised.

"It will cost jobs and boost inflation in the next few years and lower our standard of living in the long run. The uncertainty alone is already causing harm," said Macklem, repeating some of the warnings from the central bank's decision last month to reduce the policy rate by a quarter point to 3%.

Due to trade-policy uncertainty, business executives are scaling back or deferring investment plans, stopping hiring, and revising lower their sales outlook, surveys and interviews indicate.

The central bank updated its modelling on the impact of Trump tariffs and Canadian retaliation, based on the President's executive order signed Feb. 1. Macklem said based on the executive order, Canadian output would decline by about 3% over two years. Furthermore, exports would fall 8.5% in the year after tariffs take effect; consumer spending would drop by over 2% by mid-2027; and business investment would decline by roughly 12% by early 2026.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Germany's Stock Market Is on Fire. How the Election Can Drive It Higher. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Brian Swint

Germany heads to the polls this weekend and the election may bring about big changes that could give the country's red-hot stock market an extra leg up.

While the immediate outcome may not be decisive or lead to any short-term boost for the economy, it could be the beginning of the solutions to Germany's wide-ranging problems.

Europe's largest economy finds itself in an odd juxtaposition. Politically and economically, it's in crisis — the election is coming months earlier than it had to after the current government collapsed over disagreements about the budget. The country hasn't seen any meaningful growth since the end of the Covid-19 pandemic. And yet the blue-chip DAX index is booming, up 12% this year and hitting a record high on Feb. 19.

"While the upcoming German elections may not deliver sweeping policy changes, the resulting policy clarity could serve as a much-needed tailwind for German equities," said Michael Field, an equity strategist at Morningstar. "Autos, defense, and utilities are poised to be impacted from targeted support and renewed investor confidence, creating potential for long-term growth."

Right-Wing Risk

This weekend the political problems will start to be fixed. The voting process is very different from the U.S. presidential system. It relies on the parties with the most representatives working together to form a functioning administration.

Opinion polls show that Germany will almost certainly get a new chancellor to replace Olaf Scholz, with the center-right CDU Party well ahead in the polls. But it will probably have to work with its traditional center-left foe, Scholz's SPD, to form a government.

There could be surprises — CDU leader Friedrich Merz, the likely next chancellor, may have to bring in a third party such as the Greens to secure a robust majority in the Bundestag. The more would not be the merrier, because the more parties there are in negotiations, the harder it is to reach agreement. That was one of the problems with the three-party coalition that collapsed under Scholz.

The biggest risk is that the far-right AfD Party performs well enough to be able to block the new agenda. Polls show it's likely to become the second-biggest group in the parliament but all the others have said they won't work with the party, so it's very unlikely to be a part of the next government. Still, if the AfD wins enough support, it could make things difficult for the new administration.

Trade Troubles

In any case, the talks to form the coalition are likely to take months, so little will be resolved even once the votes are counted. The bigger question is how the new government can help the economy.

Here, Germany has some very big problems. For more than a decade, it has relied on cheap energy from Russia and unfettered trade with China for its prosperity. Both of those pillars have collapsed in the past few years, with Russian natural gas cut off after its invasion of Ukraine and Chinese demand dented by the country's extended property slump.

Add to that an aging population, with a shrinking workforce and growing number of dependent elderly. Then throw in the struggling manufacturing and automobiles sectors — traditional German powerhouses. What's more, U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs on imports will be damaging to countries like Germany that rely heavily on global trade. There are no quick fixes to nation's biggest issues.

"Expectations for German growth in 2025 have continued to edge lower," said Andrzej Szczepaniak, an economist at Nomura. "Confidence is remarkably below prepandemic levels across households, investors, and corporates, and is unlikely to improve noticeably in the near-term, particularly in light of U.S. policies that could hit Germany more acutely than its European neighbors."

Impressive Index

However, there is plenty of low-hanging fruit the next government could reach for that would help. The biggest issue is the so-called debt brake, a constitutional requirement that budget deficits be kept small. Merz has signaled a willingness to either reform the law or figure out some kind of workaround that would allow the government to unleash spending on roads, railways, and defense — the latter is particularly urgent given the war in Ukraine.

A CDU-led coalition would also be likely to lower taxes and work on ways to create cheaper energy, according to Maximilian Uleer, a strategist at Deutsche Bank in Frankfurt. And that would help the DAX even more.

Uleer explains the index's recent success as a function of its separation from Germany's domestic economy. He reckons that companies on the DAX make 80% of their revenue outside of the country — and the U.S. accounts for 24% of revenue. And even though traditionally strong sectors such as automobiles have been doing poorly, they make up less of the DAX than they used to. The biggest components of the index are now technology, industrials, and financials.

So the question for investors in the German election this weekend is whether the result will produce a government willing and able to do all it can to reverse the country's economic malaise. It won't be easy, but the benefits for German stocks will be great if it happens.

Write to Brian Swint at brian.swint@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: ConocoPhillips to Sell Gulf-Area Assets to Shell for $735 Million
data.description:
By Dean Seal

ConocoPhillips has agreed to sell two oilfields and a pipeline in the Gulf of Mexico to Shell subsidiaries for $735 million.

The Houston oil giant said Friday that it would sell the Ursa and Europa fields as well as the Ursa Oil Pipeline Company to Shell Offshore and Shell Pipeline Company. The deal includes an overriding royalty interest in the Ursa field.

Shell said in a separate release that with the deal, it is increasing its stake in the Ursa platform to 61.35%, a maximum limit, from 45.4%. ConocoPhillips said it would use proceeds from the sale for general corporate purposes.

The deal is expected to close by the end of the second quarter.

Write to Dean Seal at dean.seal@wsj.com
